CURRENT AFFAIRS.

The ““Irish Times ' is still in a weak but most
reprehensible sort of a way  aiding and abetting
criminals; and had any prominent Nationalist paper
during the’Land War taken up an aftitude towards
landlord shooting  similar to Arnott’s paper attitude
toward the Belfast treason mongers it would promptly
be brought to. book. Arnott’s paper aids and
encourages the Belfast and other bluffers by crying
out its alleged- belief that they will fight. — Alleged
belief in the assertion that they will fight is put up
like a scarecrow as. ‘‘the grimmest, fact of contem-
porary politics.”” - In its” leading article=on Saturday
it says:— ; i iy

‘We Unionists of the South, who are, in“a sense, *
spectators ofe the ‘situation, “canyield " to' Mr.
Churchill: and his: allies, Radicaland; Nationalist, s
ery point which.they~attempt:to make;:and: yet'=
know that Ulster holds the winning card. -Liet us

admit, in the first place, that Ulster’s fears are
groundless. We may go on to declare that, even
if these beliefs are well grounded, Ulster would
have no moral right to attempt resistance to Home
Rule. We may protest, with Mr. Churchill, that
such an attempt is the first step towards red ruin
and the general break-up of laws. - Let us even
call upon Mr. Bonar Law to plead guilty to the
charge of ‘‘ incitement to treason ’’; let us agree
that he has forfeited his right to be the respon-
sible leader of a constitutional party. Suppose,
then, that we make our opponents a present of
all these arguments—what then? The situation
is not altered by a jot. Rightly or wrongly,
Ulster intends to resist. ~She would resist if Mr.
Bonar Law took back his words to-morrow, and
the whole Unionist Party repudiated her. — Mr.
Churchill has dropped the plea of *bluff.”
Statesmen have to deal with sterner fhings than
sophistries, They have to deal with facts.
Ulster’s attitude 1s a fact—the grimmest fact of
contemporary politics. ~We have yet to learn in
what manner, if the necessity should ever arise,
Ministers propose to deal with that fact.

During the land war what would be thought of an
alleged respectable paper if it argued that though it
might admit all that could be said against murdering
landlords, that it knew that the landlord murderers
held the winning cards? The iniquity of killing land:
lords did not alter the situation by a jot, as rightly or
wrongly the men with the blunderbuss intend to shoot
the landlords. The murders will go on, however the
country may repudiate them, and statesmen have to
deal with sterner things than sophistries. They have
to deal with facts. And landlord-killing is a fact—
the grimmest fact of contemporary politics. It has
yet to learn in what manner ministers propose to deal
with the fact.

What would be thought of a Nationalist paper that
wrote like that in 1879? And yet the ‘writing in the
““ Irish Times '’ is quite parallel. Notwithstanding
the bout of *‘ Civil War *’ that we have experienced in
the Belfast atrocities, the *‘ Irish Times '’ goes on, as
we have quoted, as a sort of advance agent -for
encouraging ‘the murderers and traitors. It is, of
course, very silly writing, and shows what confusion
the doctrines of the man Law, who ought to be in
prison, has helped to create in various quarters.. The
extract we have quoted from *Irish Times'' is an
encouragement of murder and treason. And its ques-
tion as to what his Majesty's Government will do. if
the murderers commence their  work—they have
already given a sample of it in Belfast—is a suggestion
that the King should hold up his hands and surrender
to Orange murderers. That in plain words is what the
““ Irish Times '’ suggests. Certain intending assassins
will murder if the King and his Parliament do a certain
thing, and the ‘ Irish Times '’ advises the King and
hig Parliament under these circumstances to hold up
hands and surrender to the intending murderers. Of
course, the King and his Parliament would not be
intimidated by, and would not-surrender to; a pack of
real assassins much less to.a pack of Orange black-

-guard ‘bluffers-whose great. prowess was characteristi-

cally displayed in the recent Belfast atrocities. The
King’s troops: were sent to Belfast recently to cool,
down ‘the ““Civil War,”” and Alf Fox put its" tail
between its legs and ran away from the Belfast

beroes.”  Tf, when the King and his Parliament

pass Home Rule, any Belfast blackguards commit
murders, of course the King's Government will take
measures to stop the progress of murder, and it ‘will
have any convicted murderers promptly hanged.

The square and compass are working in the “‘National’’
Bank, of which such alleged great patriots as Sir
Thomas Esmonde and Mr. Barry O’Brien are amongst
the directors. ~ What does the new chairman know
about Treland and her business in compavison with the
late chairman, Mr. Slattery?  Brother Alton is, we
understand, the great Pcoh-Bah at the ‘ National '’
now, and we suppose directors like Sir Thomas
Esmonde and Mr. Barry O’Brien are mere cyphers.
We have seen that Brother Alton’s son, Master Alton,
has been pitchforked into the position of Assistant In-
spector. Master Alton has not had quite twelve years’
service. The boy was promoted rapidly, and had been
accountant during the past few years in Westland Row,
living, of course, at home with his pa. . Last month
Master Alton, son of Brother Alton, the Pooh Bah of
the ‘“ National,” after eleven years’ service was
appointed Assistant Inspector! How does such gal-
loping promotion compare with the usual rate of pro-
motion in the ‘‘ National ’ Bank? What are we to
think of the chairman and directors, including the great
patriots, Sir T. Esmonde, M.P., and Mr. Bany
O’Brien, who sanctioned such a proceeding? The
idea of appointing Master Alton, a comparative strip-
ling, we ‘presume, for he has only eleven years’ service,
to the onerous and responsible duties of an.inspector,
sending hira to inspect branches run by men old enough
to be his grandfather! There was a new branch of the
“‘ National ” opened recently at Ballygar in the County
Roscommon, and Brother Alton had the appointing of
a manager in his hands. Who was appointed?  The
son of the manager in Roscommon, and, needless to
say, he is one of the “ saved.” Colour right—all right.
Do Sir T. Esmonde, M.P., and Mr. Bairry O’Brien
take any interest in the Banlk, or are they only ornamen-
tal directors? We will probably have more to say about
the conduct of this ‘‘ National Bank *’ with its head
office in merry England.

A very ugly drink tragedy occwrred in the district of

Gorey, Co. Wexford, on a recent Sunday. Seven or

eight men from the country on a recent Sunday even-

ing congregated in the bungery of John Stafford, Main

Street, Gorey. = At the drunkery of the man Stafford,

of Main Street, Gorey, seven of these apparently drank

to excess, and Stafford has the profits, one of the men

is now in eternity, and two of the othersare on remand,

charged with his death—nice wages for a Sunday drink-

ing bout in Stafford’s drunkery, but Stafford is not in

the dock. This low bungery business is an awful thing.

These men were apparently kindly fellows, meeting on

friendly terms, but some of them were maddened by

drink.  What a hellish trade this of making profits out

of the weakness of unfortunate labouring men who

drink irrationally. A Bung is supposed to be a man of

. . at least some intelligence, indeed, many of them think
they are quite ‘‘tone,” and there might be much

urged in favour of the proposition that the Bung who

- for profit plies unfortunate, rough and unrestrained
Tabouring men with drink to excess is far more guilty

than the befuddled fellows who put down drink on top

of drink and make beasts of themselves. In this case,

one of the labourers is in eternity, two are charged with

*  being concerned in his death, and we suppose the bun-

drunk but one, who is what they called a  wine-

man a sister of the deceased, weeping loudly, ran into
the room. She said she had come from Dublin (;h::t
morning and only got the telegram about her brother’s
death at half-past eight o’clock that morning. She
cried frantically and said they need not have killed and
murdered the poor boy if they were drunk itself. He
was o _harmless, inoffensive boy who would not offend
a child. His blood was all along the road and it was
a horible sight.  She wanted to know where was the
publichouse that gave these men drink to set them
drunk on a Sunday night like that. It was a shame
and a disgrace to set men mad in that way to fight and
kill each other. In Dublin no publichouse would be
opened for anyone after five o’clock, but, if this was
the kind of thing that was carried on in Gorey half of
the drink shops in the town ought to be shut up. We
sympathise with this outburst of the killed man’s
sister, though we fear she was not accurate in stating
that no publichouse in Dublin would be opened for
anyone after five o’clock, This case is very -dis-
graceful to Gorey. Why is not -the man
Stafford, who supplied these men with drink to
excess, proceeded against? ~ What are the police of
Gorey doing?  Are they qualifying for Bung engi-
neered testimonials when they may happen to be
shifted ?

At the last meeting of the East Limerick Executive
of the United Irish League, E. Mitchell, M.C.C.,
proposed and it was passed unanimously :—

‘“That we, the members of the East Limerick
Executive of the United Irish League call upom
the Protestant shopkeepers and traders and others
in the county and city of Limerick, who had always
been treated most courteously and kindly by their
Catholic fellow-countrymen, and who have made
fortunes in the business of the Catholic popula-
tion, to come out at once and repudiate the
cowardly and brutal attacks made on the brave
workers of Belfast by the Orange brigands and
their leaders. We trust that the good feelings which
have always existed between Catholic and Pro-
testant in Ireland will continue; but at the same
time we feel it is the duty of those gentlemen who
profess the same religion as those Ulster bravadoes
to condemn these base outrages at Belfast.”

This is a very proper resolution, and we hear that it is
contemplated getting up a petition in Limerick for the
signature of Protestant shopkeepers and others. It
ought not have been left to the United Irish League of
Tast Limerick to call upon local Protestant shop-
keepers and traders to repudiate the blackguards, ‘as
these people ought to have acted on their own initiative.,
IBven now that they are invited to repudiate the black-
guards, we doubt if some of them will be anxious $o
respond. In these circumstances it may be well to
give them an opporbunity of signing, or refusing to sign,
a condemnation of the work of the Belfast murderous
blackguards. A collection, or series of collections, for
the victims of the Belfast atrocities is being made all
over the East Limerick constituency, and the Parlia:

On the occasion of the London Irish Literary Society’s
excursion to Kilkenny the local Gaelic League arranged
a concert and dramatic performance. During the whole
- performance the players were subjected to a series of

| unmannerly and vulgar interruptions. A veterinary
s surgeon, we hear, was one of the interrupters.  This
suggests & new form of mouth disease that the profes-
sion should at once investigate.

The following letter appeared in a recent issue of the

Belfast ** Irish News " :

DEar S1r,—There are some amongst us who have
begun to discover from experience that two can
play at a nasty game just as readily as two can
be friendly and neighbourly if they are sensible
people, blessed with an ordinary share of common-
sense, no matter how they differ in religion or
politics.

I returned to Belfast last night from a tour in
the Midlands and West of Ireland. I started
business at Mullingar, and travelled  along to-
Multyfarnham, Crossdoney, Cavan, Ballyhaise,
and Clones; thence West, through Enniskillen to
Manorhamilton, Collooney, and Sligo town; then
south by the G. S. and W. Railway line to Tubber-
curry, Claremorris, Tuam, Athenry and Galway;
and I travelled home by Athlone and Mullingar
again to Dublin and Belfast:

‘Without troubling your readers as to -any parti-
culars regarding what happened me at various
places, and not giving any indication of my iden-
tity, I may say that for six orders I got on & pre-
vious journey over the same route, I got one on
this occasion; and for every £10 worth of business
I brought home when I covered this ground last
year, I did not bring £1 back to my firm last night.
I had written from various places telling them
how matters stood; and in consequence of the
civil, but chilling, reception I got everywehere I
knew there was no ill-feeling against myself; but
still the shopkeepers simply declined to do busi-
ness.

It would be impossible for me to earn a living,
depending as I am on commission, in those parts
of the country. I have been successfully travel-
ling for the past 13 years—my first journey to the
South was made in June, 1899. Therefore, I
have decided to accept an offer made by a Dublin
firm on my way home. Many of my colleagues
on the road have had similar experiences since the
‘“ irresponsible youths '’ began to give Belfast a
bad name—without the country hearing a word
of condemnation from the responsible Unionist
citizens of such infamous conduct. I may add I am
a Protestant, and was not a Home Ruler, or very
much of anything, up to the month of July.—

Yours truly, D1sGUSTED COMMERCIAL.
August 9th.

Carson, Bonar Law and others should have been
arrested long ago.  But, in default of proceeding in
that way with these treason-mongers, it is well at least
that Mr. Churchill has taken them publicly to task.
We have been pointing out the anarchy and lawlessness
that Bonar Law and Carson were standing for, and we
think the Government showed great weakness in not
putting them on their trial. In July we pointed out
that thirty years ago had the *‘ Irish Times’’ been a
Nationalist paper, it would have been suppressed and
its editor probably put on a plank bed for preaching
open treason and lawlessness.  The impunity with
which the Government allowed the Carson gang to
carry on their campaign encouraged alike the Belfast
I blackguards and Arnott’s ‘‘ Times.’”

The Orange blackguards of Belfast began their *“ civil
war ’’ rather early for their purposes, and the *‘ Irish
Times >’ was driven to utter a lip-denunciation. The

{7l dressing down which Churchill has given Bonar Law,
though rather belated, will probably make others be-
- sides Law pull in their horns.  Churchill writes of

be accompanied in England by the lynching of his
Majesty’s Ministers.

But these speeches which he and Sir Tdward

ment, but incitements to the Orangemen, and as
the detestable incidents which have lately taken
place in Belfast prove, they have been only too

Mr. Churchill’s point that if the people are against
Home Rule they can repeal it or modify it in the in-

The number of Catholic Colleges falling into line
with the Trish Ireland idea indicates that the Irish
idea in educationin Ireland is gaining ground steadily,
if not as quickly as one would wish. St. Joseph's
College, Ballinasloe, which is under the patronage and
supervision of the Most Rev, Dr. Gilmartin, Bishop of
Cloufert, announces that Ivish is the language of the
class-room, chapel, refectory and playground. We
hear on good authority that the College this year is to
rear up a new school of pipers according to the old
tradition, the famous Denny Delaney being the
teacher.  The College has a fife and drum band—
Irish music only; and it is intended to have a war
pipers’ band also.  The Irish language is taught to
all the students, and hwling, handball, and Gaelic
football are encouraged. St. Joseph’s claims to have

is paid.to the development of character on manly and
patriotic lines.” On the prospectus the details of the
College outfit is prefaced by the following sugges-

provided with at least the following articles, which
can all be had of Irish manufacture.””  In another
column a list of scholarships offered Ly the College will
be found, and any further information and prospectus

THE NORWAY INSULT.

N OTWITHSTANDING the formal and representa-

tive protests of the Dublin Corporation and the
Dublin County Council, the Home Rule *‘ Liberal **
Government persist in their purpose of dumping one
Norway over here to be Secretary of the Irish Post
Office,  We fail to see any real campaign in the
editorial columns of the so-called Nationalist Dublin
dailies against this unwarranted insult to Ireland on
the part of the Government, and of course the ‘¢ Irish
Times ” has no protest, as even a point against the
“ Liberal "’ Government is naturally waived when it
sins in favour of an English importation. It is now
said that the appointment is to be an interim one for a
period of two years. We suppose this announcement

I8 a sort of bending before the Ivish indignation that

the news of the impending dump aroused. But the

objection against importing an Englishman for two

Jyears 1s as great as importing him for life to:be Seere-

fary of a department that is to be handed over to Irish

control by the Bill that the ‘‘ Liberal "’ Government
1s committed to make part of the law if it lasts long
enough.  If it were honestly intended to dump this
man over here for two years the dump remains quite
as objectionable, whilst the act increases in silliness;
and, needless to say, the two years’ business only
- increases our suspicions that an unscrupulous job is
on hand.

What deranged mind conceived the project of
importing an Englishman to be Secretary of the Irish
Post Office for two years? We are driven to the con-
clusion that there is method in the madness, and once
the fellow gets a footing here the. difficulties of shifting
him will be found so difficult that he will be turned
into a permanent dump,

We don’t know who's brother-in-law, or cousin, or

uncle, this man Norway may be, but if the Govern-
ment want to find promotion for him Ireland is not
the place for him. = The Government'’s case is that
Ireland is fit to take over control of a large part of its
affairs, including the Post Office, and in face of that
My, Samuel, a prominent Home Rule British Minister,
insults Ireland, and in a measure belies his own con-
tention that Ireland is fit and ready to deal with its
own Post Office, by selecting an Inglishman to be
Secretary of the Tvish Post Office.  The thing is most
insulting and intolerable. ~ When Asquith came over
here we were not able to heartily cheer, though we were
glad, for various reasons, that he got an enthusiastic
reception—the cheers were for a Bill that had been
acknowledged but had not yet been paid. A pretty
sequel to all the cheering and the torchlights and to
Asquith’s speeches is that his colleague, Samuel, turns
round and dumps, or seeks to dump, an Englishman
into the Secretaryship of the Irish Post Office. Samuel
is supposed to be a financier, whatever that is, and
perhaps he wants a chum over here to make out the
accounts, from the English point of view, of the Irish
Post Office; we don’t know. Whatever the object,
whether Norway is intended to make out the best case
against Ireland, or ether he has claims on some
members of the British Government, whatever the
cause of the attempt to foist him over here over the
heads of Irish postal officials, the attempt is a direct
insult to Ireland; and the Irish Party should resent it
as one man. The fact that the ** Freeman *’ editorially
appears rather shy in the protest line appears rather
ominous, and the fact that the affair is being rushed
whilst Parliament is not sitting looks rather
suspicious.

The suggestion that Norway is fo be kept for two
years only adds to the insult. Why dump him over
here at all if he is to be shot back as an undesirable
in two years’ time? Tor two years the Irish officials
will be teaching him his business, and by the time that
he is able to pronounce correctly Youghal, Tipperary

and Drogheda, . his period of service—if we are to
believe the two year yarn, which personally we do not
believe—will be “about up and oft he trots to England.
If anyone on the eve of Asquith’s visit suggested
that this trick would be attempted in the following
month he would be regarded as a factionist prophet of
evil.  Surely the leaders of the Irish Parliamentary,
Party, or if necessary all members of that Party,
could send a written protest in the name of Ireland
to the proper quarter against the perpetration of this
insult to Irish administrative ability. Before such a
probest the * Liberal ” Party would have to drop this
job and act consistently and in consonance with its
own professions concerning Ireland’s ability to manage
affaivs.  The thinking Irish public, we believe, are
y indignant over this matter, and the silence, or
next to silence, of the ““ Freeman >’ will not meet the
case. The country looks to the Irish Party to tell the
Government that Ireland will not have Norway.

HIS is the Holiday Month, and we feel that we

cannot do better than give our readers a few
hints which no doubt .will enable them to derive full
benefit from their period of rest and recreation. Holi-
day-takers may be divided into two classes: those who
go home and those who leave home.. The first class
onsists mainly of school-children, University or other
tudents, and tlie large numbers of country-folk en-
aged in work in our cities. With these we have no
oncern other than to console them with the reflection
1ab after all there is mo place like home—on this side
of the Jordan. In the second class are those to whom
our little words of advice and help will, we feel sure,
prove welcome.
In the first place comes the important matter of
shubting-up one’s house. The wisdom of notifying to
I burglars and house-breakers in the neighbourhood
that the house has been left derelict always suggests
itself at once to the careful householder. ~ This may.
be done, of course, by pulling-down the blinds, but a
better and more conspicuous plan is to put brown paper
in the windows. This dispels any allusion that the
unsuspecting burglar may entertain as to the closed-
up appearance being merely an outward token of in-
ward grief, and by adopting this simple and inexpensive
device many a hard-working house-breaker is helped to
a much needed job. ~

encourage them to go home. Otherwise they may give
entertainments in your absence, and their hospitality
is certain to cause reflections to be made on your lack
of a similar quality. Dogs should be taken with you as
they may cause annoyance where you are stopping, and
this will secure you respect and civility,  Cats it is

A FLEMISH REVIVALIST ON THE ORO sceite
; san-miciL.

HAVING been asked to write about the F[egush

Revival, and especially about vur College Guilds,
which, thank God, have now been introduced into Ire-
land also, I hesitated for some time, on account of the
difficulty which a.stranger experiences of writing Eng-
lish and also because of my insufficient knowledge of
the present conditions of the Irish people. Kvery
organisatign must be appropriated to the national spirit,
and although the principles are everywhere the same,
yet their application may differ from nation to nation.
But my sincere love for Ireland overbalanced these
considerations. I have no doubt that my readers will
be indulgent, and only take into account my great
desire to see the Irish of the twentieth century a real
Irish people, free from all foreign alloy and existing by
itself.

The institution then of College Guilds is founded on
an indisputable principle, which is, that it is necessary
to inculcate into the mind of the young the principles
which must regulate their conduct throughout life.
Could there exist a patriotic people if the walls of the
infant school did not resound to the strains of national
hymns? Could there exist a religious, a God-fearing
people, if masters did not teach their pupis to pray?
Assuredly not. World-wide experience proves the con-
trary. Is it not remarkable that many learned Angli-
cans preserve throughout life the frivolous prejudices
of their childhood against the Catholic Church ?  The
basis of the present struggle in Belgium between Catho-
lics and Free Thinkers is the school, because the
enemies of our faith well know that an early Catholic
education is an immovable foundation and a pledge of
loyalty to the Church. In unbelieving countries also,
missionaries ave especially careful to take into consi-
deration the evangelisation of the children, because
they may hope for a more abundant harvest from this
sort of labour than from that of converting adults.

The heart of a child is as a fertile soil, and principles
once planted therein.remain, as it were, mecrusted in
marble. The tree shoots up in the direction which the
plant has received. You see then that it 1s extremely
necessary to take good care of the education of your
youth if you wish to produce a real Irish people. The
flower of Irish youth you will find in the colleges of your
land. Nothing, then, can be more useful in provoking
the revival of the Irish language and spirit. than College
Guilds.

Youth is susceptible of enthusiasm, and without this
enthusiasm it is impossible to obtain any great work.

But enthusiasm has dangers, and the enthusiasm of °

youtl is often inconsiderate. Youth cries when it should
be quiet, and so on. Tt must therefore be guided.

The reef against which the endeavours of many right-
thinking Flemings were broken was a certain wanb of

Hatred destroys; love builds. The aim of the Irish
Revival must not be to wage war upon English, but

Irish rights.

Flemish alone will never suffice for the Flemings,
because it is not an international language. We shall
alw have need of another tongue, German, English,
or French, and above all French, not beeause it is the
most useful for commercial purposes or for studies, but
because it is the language of a part of our countrymen.
Trish also is not an international language, and business
men will always have need of another tongue. On ac-
count of its geographical and political position, English
must be the second one for you, but Irish must be the
language.of your nation. Without its national idiom
it is impossible for a nation to develop itself, for the
anguage is, as it were, a great deal of the nation itself.
To educate your people in a foreign language is as ib
were to transplant your dear little shamrock into a
foreign soil : it will never flourish so well as in its native

an impossibility. German is the second language in

takes the first place.

result. In Flemish Belgium the first language must
be Flemish; in Ireland the first must be Irish. Of
course when Irish will be thoroughly the national speech

is no obstacle. These will never need linglish, and

they live very comfortably. The same thing may exist

gians. ‘‘ The future belongs to the youth!'’ sang our

being often cool the love of home and fatherland. But
you have this in your fayour: the minds of Trishmen

recent successful strike for a shilling per week increase

machinery and employed less hands.. Thus a spinning
frame which engaged, say, six workers, now has to do
with  three; these three get an extra dole of sixpence
each per week, and the generous mill-owners pocket
the wages of the other three hands, minus a sixpence
each! The result is that the girls are driven literally

go to such an occupation, but the replies invariably
are, that ‘‘ they have been, as it were, bred to the
work, and that it was not so bad some time ago.”” In
the meantime the holocaust of womanly life goes on
without comment; labour is cheap, dirt cheap, in the
North, and indeed it is only the robust and healthy
who are wanted for the work. Should adelicate
anemic, city-reaved girl feel her powers failing she

on the first opportunity; and as the spinner is unfitted
for any other occupation, the mill must be faced again.

she bore her sufferings.  Very glad was she to die,

THE INTERMEDIATE SYSTEM.
By . Hucues DowLinG.

Y" OUR contributor, Dr. Laffan, is interesting from

3 & personal point of view—he is one of the few
Irish Catholic laymen, not connected with education,
who writes on the subject; and, however much we may
differ with him in his views and in his method of
settling this question, we deem his articles worthy of
a veply. It would tend to malke our self-imposed task
the easier if thé Dr. would read and copy the language
of Sir Lucius O'Trigger. A candid friend may be a
useful, but he is certainly not an agreeable, companion.
So with your contributor. I do not appraise him the
higher because he styles Intermediate teachers
‘‘ journeymen '’ and ‘‘ hacks.””  Surely the Dr. can
display his opinion without wounding™ the suscepti-
bilities of poor us.

Dr. Laffan, in his article in the LEADER of August
10th, treats us to the history of the introduction of the
Intermediate system, and informs us naively of the in-
cffectual parts he played therein. I pass this in silence
as not of living interest and deal with some more vital
points. Our Dr. states that there is at present a grow-
ing volume of opinion against the Intermediate system
and that we want a commission of inquiry into its work-
ing.  There has been a strong opinion against it for
twenty years, but what we want to settle the question
is not a commission to inquire into its defects,
.which are notorious, but an Act of Parliament
to enable the Commissioners to give grants on
inspection, and not _for the results examinations.
To the satisfaction of most people interested in
this subject, Mr. Birrell promises to grant us this im-
provement next October, but still the Dr. is not happy
because it will not remove the inequality between
Catholic and Protestant endowments. I fear me he
does not keep his ideas well pigeon-holed, and so mixes
up two different aspects of the same question, confuses
himself without illuminating his readers. This is to
do scant justice to his intelligence.

When the Intermediate education system was intro-
duced there was great reason for complaint against
the unequal endowment of Catholics and Protestants,
but the Endowed Schools Commission has since sat and
settled much of this inequality. Moreover, the
National University has done something to satisfy
Catholic claims, so we have now much less reason to
complain. Besides, it is not for the Intermediate
Commissioners to interfere with this matter; their’s to
administer, as best they may, the Intermediate Educa-
tion Act. i g

T now come to the part of Dr. Laffan’s a.rhqle which
interests me most—his references to everything con-
nected with lay assistant teachers, apd I have no hesi-
tation in saying that it is the holding and acting on
ideas, such as he gives utterance to, that more _ths.n
aught else tends to keep. the whole tone of Trish middle
class life so wanting in initiative and conseguently so
unprogressive. The idea unfortunately obtains rather
widely in Ireland that, because the Intermediate_tea-
cher is often poorly educated, always unj;ru.mecL
wretehedly paid, and worse respected, his lot in other
countries where things are mapped according to sound
principles of justice and intelligence is _s1m11a}'. ; N((i
mistalke could be greater and more misleading; an

~ nothing would give me greater pleasure and would be

easier to refute it by’ apposite .exump!es and appropriate
quotation.  But your space is precious, and so T con-
‘tent myself in dealing with the question briefly.

What is the function of an Int.ermedmte teacher?
o fit his pupil for the battle of life as fully as time
and eireumstances: permit. What must be the char-
“racter and the education of the médn that is able to do
this adequately? Both must be of & high order. How.
d of these qualities:to be obtained for
the position of teacher? By respecting those already
in these positions; by giving them salaries enabling

THE LEADER.

them to occupy positions in the social world as high as.

AuGust 24, 1919,

doctors, solicitors and: other men of University rank. -
And, lastly, by holding out to them the prospects of
increase of salary and fixity of tenure during satisfac-
tory discharge. = Where do these conditions obtain?
In all the different states of Germany, in France, Bel-
gium, and Holland. And, in short, in any country
where the importance of the functions of the secondary
teacher is understood. Do these conditions obtain in
Ireland? Not at all; quite the contrary. Here the
secondary teacher is poorly educated, offen not
having as much knowledge as would enable him to.
pass the matriculation examinationof a University with".
honours; he is always untrained ; he is poorly remuner-
ated, and he has no fixity of tenure. He is, in short,
neither self-supporting nor self-respecting. - Result asg
to the teacher: His is always a short stay in the school-
room, and that to tho accompaniment of a grumble.
As to the pupil: a poor training for life and little educa-

tion. The teacher that is not respected in his calling, -

and who is always feeling the pinch of poverty, is ill
calculated to perform his onerous duties with that
virility ‘which educates while training. As to the
nation: an unpreparedness for the battle of dife and &
want of that initiative which begets progress and ulti-
mate success.

From the low financial and social position of the lay
secondary teacher there springs up for him in the mind
of the man in the street feelings which frequently dis-
play themselves in condescension and toleration, buft
never proper respect; and which occasionally blossom
forth in the unblushing use of the discourteous terms of
‘“ journeyman ” and ‘‘ hack.”’ This, my readers, is a
true billl And we were once known as insula doc-
torum! Ah, me, qualis erat tantum mutatus ab illo
Hictore !

Dr. Laffan’s views on the question of tenure are
most incomprehensible.  He thinks we should be in
the position of drapers’ assistants and not in that of
dispensary doctors or hospital nurses under a board
of guardians. Now, if the teacher is ever to be of real
power in developing the intellect of the country he
must have such terms of tenure as will protect him
against disturbance during satisfactory discharge of
duty and which will prevent his dismissal at all times
without a written statement of the causes of removal,
and with the right of being heard in his own defence
before an independent court of appeal. This is &
point which afiects the nation more than the teacher.
The State always. has as teachers the kind of
instructors it desires and deserves. f the position
of the teacher is one to be envied, as regards salary,
pension and tenure, by the clever young man about to
select a career in life, then he will work to gain it; but
if on the contrary it is unattractive on these points,
then he will give it a wide berth, and the country; to
its incalculable loss, will have to put up men of second
or even third rate powers as the educators of its rising
generation.  This latter up to this has been Ireland’s
lot; and with unexampled want of intelligence this is
the lot Dr. Laffan wishes to perpetuate for her. But,
fortunately for the country, wiser counsels are likely
to ol;ta.in; and when the new conditions of service
promised by Mr. Birrell, and shortly to be introduced
in Parliament, have been finally settled, it will be
found that the terms are so satisfactory that we, the
lay secondary teachers, will be enabled to command
that respect and consideration our arduous and im-
portant duties merit, then it will be impossible for our
Doctor, and those who think like him, to consider and
treat us with the well-feigned condescension of old;
then we shall be placed in our proper position of
educated gentlemen and entitled to the respect and:
consideration always accorded to the professional
classes. 2 X

Dr. Laffan thinks that we, ‘and those trying to im-
prove our status in every respect, put forward the
demand for money from the Government on the ples
that we are the assistant teachers of Treland, Herein
the Doctor ig labouring under a hallucination. The
demand for the Parlismentary Grant up to the present
has: been, and is; for the improvement of the laz

1
1:

- assistant masters and mistresses. This is very clearly
stated in the resolutions of the Catholic Bishops,
Eassed in June, 1910. And rightly so, as this want
as for some years held pride of p]ucc in urgency;
and 16 is to supply it in part that Mr. Bivrell has, with
fhe consent of tlie Catholic headmasters, obtained, in
_ the way of a Parliamentary Grant, the sum of €40, 000
- a year for Irish lay secondary toachers.  This plncw
us for the first time in the eye of the l(\w, recognises
our wants, and supplies them, and so raises our pro-
fession fo that of a career worthy of the aspirations
- and abilities of many a clever man who before gave
the position of Irish lu\ secondary teaching the go-by.
In so acting Mr. Birrell deserves well of Lho Trish
people, for he has now thereby conferred great and
, lasting benefits on the third main bmmh of Irish
education.

As to what this money is
to, I can state little definitely.
pend, I know, on qualifications and experience;
but we are a motley crew, varying in age from
18 to 70; and in education or knowledge from the
inability to pass the matriculation of a university to
possession of the highest honours at the M.A. exami-
nations. To allot the salaries of such unequal
claimants is a_ difficuit but not impossible: task, and
one naturally demanding time and consideration; but
that once settled the rest will be easy. All future
teachers must be men with recognised qualifications
as regards education, training and experience, who will,
each in his own clqss beﬂm his career at the lowest
rung of the ladder, and \vho in the case of honour
and\mtes, will with success in his calling rise to the
maximum of £300 a year. This to our Doctor will
possibly appear quite too high;; but to anyone who is

+ at all a bit of a statesman it is but the first step
towards putting us in our right position as important
functionaries for Ireland’s regeneration.
